Your voice in Parliament and your champion in Wakefield & Rothwell. It’s my honour and privilege to represent over 73,000 people living in the Wakefield and Rothwell constituency as your Member of Parliament (MP). It’s my job to make sure that your voice is heard in Parliament and since being elected I’ve supported over 12,000 constituents with a wide range of casework. You can read more about the kind of things I can help with here . ​ One of the best parts of the job is getting out an about by visiting hundreds of local businesses, community groups and supporting some of our fantastic local events. So, if you’ve got something you’d like me to come along to, please get in touch! Read more about Simon Facebook Posts: Latest News: 50 new buses for Wakefield Great to join Arriva Yorkshire in Wakefield today as they unveiled the first of their brand‑new, Yorkshire‑built buses -part of a £340m investment to modernise their UK fleet, with £15m of that spent right here at home Wakefield is the first to benefit, with 50 new lower‑emission buses already entering service. It’s a real boost for passengers and a sign of the progress being made to improve public transport locally. There’s still more to do before services are where our com Mar 15 1 min read Should we ban social media for under-16's? I’m really keen to hear your views on an issue many parents and young people have raised with me: how we can help young people build a healthier, safer relationship with phones and social media. The Government has launched a national conversation on children’s online safety, recognising both the huge benefits of technology and the growing concerns about its impact on young people’s wellbeing. Feb 16 1 min read Happy birthday Harry! Wakefield WW2 Veteran Celebrates 100th Birthday with Visit from Local MP and Messages from the King and Prime Minister A much‑loved Wakefield resident and Second World War veteran has celebrated his 100th birthday with a series of heartfelt tributes recognising his remarkable life and service. Harry Birdsall, who served in the Royal Army Service Corps and helped evacuate prisoners from the Bergen‑Belsen concentration camp in 1945, marked his milestone birthday surrounded by Feb 11 2 min read Reflecting on 2025 As we begin the new year, I want to take a moment to reflect on all that has been achieved in 2025, the first full calendar year of this Labour Government, and to look ahead to what we can accomplish together in 2026. The General Election may feel like only yesterday, but already this Government has delivered transformative change for communities here in Wakefield and Rothwell. Over the past twelve months, we have passed crucial pieces of legislation that will make a real dif Dec 28, 2025 2 min read Wakefield city centre needs a Post Office. The Post Office branch at Trinity Walk is set to close at the end of February, following a decision by TG Jones, the business where the branch is currently located. ​ I am working closely with Wakefield Council, the Post Office, Wakefield BID, and other local organisations to fight for a continued Post Office presence in the city centre - where it belongs. I am also extremely concerned about the impact this decision will have on branch staff, especially so close to Christmas Dec 7, 2025 1 min read A Budget that delivers for Wakefield and Rothwell Last week, the Chancellor delivered her Budget, which carried a strong message: Britain’s future will be built on fairness, not austerity. Gone are the days of cuts that hollowed out our communities or promises that couldn’t be paid for. pages.title: About | Simon Lightwood MP
pages.meta_description: Simon Lightwood MP is the Labour and Co-operative Member of Parliament for Wakefield and Rothwell
headings: h3: ​Simon is considered both a Wakefield and Leeds MP.
h3: ​Simon is considered both a Leeds and a Wakefield MP.
pages.text_excerpt: --> About | Simon Lightwood MP top of page Home About Advice & Support Visit Parliament Gallery Transport Contact More Use tab to navigate through the menu items. About. About Simon Election Results Wakefield MPs Leeds MPs Constituency About Simon Simon Lightwood is the Labour and Co-operative Member of Parliament for Wakefield and Rothwell. ​ He was first elected the MP for Wakefield in a by-election held on 23 June 2022 with a majority of 4,925 (17.9%) winning the seat back for the Labour Party, following the resignation of the disgraced Conservative MP, Imran Ahmad Khan. ​ ​​​​ In Parliament he has campaigned on a wide range of issues, including bus services, NHS dentistry and antisocial behaviour in line with the priorities he set out in his election campaign. ​ In October 2022 he was asked to join the Labour’s front bench as a Shadow Transport Minister, which included responsibility for buses, taxis and coaches. ​ In September 2023 he was re-appointed as the Shadow Minister for Local Transport with an expanded portfolio to include buses, taxis, light rail, active travel, future of transport and Transport For London. Simon was re-elected for the new constituency of Wakefield and Rothwell on 4 July 2024 with a majority of 9,246 (22.9%). He was then invited by the new Prime Minister, Sir Keir Starmer MP, to serve as Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State (Minister for Local Transport) in the Department for Transport. ​ The Prime Minister asked Simon to continue to serve as Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State ( Minister for Roads and Buses) in the September 2025 reshuffle. ​ Prior to being elected, Simon was a proud NHS worker and was previously a Labour councillor and cabinet member for public health. ​ He studied theatre acting at Bretton Hall College in Wakefield, after moving to the city in the late nineties. He bought his first home and met his husband whilst living in Wakefield. ​ Simon lives in Wakefield with his husband and their two young children. He is a proud Wakefield Trinity fan. Downloadable portraits Election Results ​ 2024 General Election: ​ ​ ​ ​ ​ ​ ​ ​ ​ ​ ​ ​ ​ ​​​ ​ 2022 Wakefield By-Election: Wakefield MPs ​ ​ ​Simon is considered both a Wakefield and Leeds MP. ​ Your MP is determined by where you live. The UK is divided into 650 constituencies, each represented by one MP. Your MP can only assist people who live within their own constituency, so it's important to make sure you're reaching out to the correct MP for your address. You can enter your postcode here to check. There are actually four MPs which cover the Wakefield. ​ ​ Simon Lightwood MP - Wakefield and Rothwell: Includes Rothwell, Stanley and Outwood East, Wakefield East, Wakefield North, Wakefield West, and Wrenthorpe and Outwood West. Yvette Cooper MP - Pontefract, Castleford and Knottingley: Covers Airedale and Ferry Fryston, Altofts and Whitwood, Castleford, Central and Glasshoughton, Knottingley, Pontefract North, and Pontefract South. Jade Botterill MP - Ossett and Denby Dale: Includes Denby Dale, Horbury and South Ossett, Kirkburton, Ossett, Wakefield Rural, and Wakefield South. Jon Trickett MP - Normanton and Hemsworth: Covers Ackworth, North Elmsall and Upton, Crofton, Ryhill and Walton, Featherstone, Hemsworth, Normanton, South Elmsall, and South Kirkby ​ ​ Leeds MPs ​ ​ ​Simon is considered both a Leeds and a Wakefield MP. ​ Your MP is determined by where you live. The UK is divided into 650 constituencies, each represented by one MP. Your MP can only assist people who live within their own constituency, so it's important to make sure you're reaching out to the correct MP for your address. You can enter your postcode here to check. There are actually ten MPs which cover the Leeds: ​ Simon Lightwood MP - Wakefield and Rothwell Includes Rothwell, Stanley and Outwood East, Wakefield East, Wakefield North, Wakefield West, and Wrenthorpe and Outwood West. ​ Hilary Benn MP - Leeds South Includes Beeston & Holbeck, Middleton Park

h3: You can find a wide range of useful and emergency contacts here ​ ​ ​
pages.text_excerpt: --> Advice & Support | Simon Lightwood MP top of page Home About Advice & Support Visit Parliament Gallery Transport Contact More Use tab to navigate through the menu items. Advice & Support. Ask for help How can we help? Help with Council issues Surgery Appointments Complete our contact form Useful contacts Ask for help Our casework service is supported by two dedicated members of staff. We’re a small team but we work really hard to support as many people as possible, as quickly as possible. Because we receive so many requests for assistance we sometimes have to prioritise urgent cases first. We'll also prioritise personal emails over any automated campaign emails. Before you get in touch it's important that you know there is a strict Parliamentary protocol which prevents me from intervening on behalf of another MP’s constituents. So please double check that you live in the Wakefield and Rothwell constituency and that I am your Member of Parliament. You can do this by entering your postcode here . How we can help? ​ ​ We can help constituents with the following issues: ​ Tax problems involving the HM Revenue and Customs Department Welfare issues dealt with by the Department for Work and Pensions such as benefits, tax credits and state pensions Issues handled by the Home Office such as immigration and passports Issues relating to the Department of Health and Social Care such as concerns about hospitals or access to treatment Any issues arising from any other government departments Issues with utility companies Consumer issues ​ We can support constituents by: ​ Writing to the Minister with responsibility for the department concerned Asking an official for an explanation of why a decision has been made Asking for a decision to be reviewed ​ So that we can help, you will need to: ​ Provide your full name Provide your full postal address including post code Provide a contact telephone number and an email address if you have one Provide your national insurance number Include scans / copies of any supporting documents (please do not enclose originals) ​ We will contact you to let you know what action Simon has taken and to advise you of the outcome of our enquiries. If you continue to be unhappy with a decision or issues raised, you may be able to refer your case to the relevant Ombudsman. We will include information on any further action you can take when we contact you. ​ ​ ​ ​​ ​ Help with council issues ​ ​ ​ Issues relating to services provided by the Local Authority, Wakefield Council and Leeds City Council need to be raised with the relevant council department or your local councillor in the first instance. ​ This includes: ​ Housing and council tax Anti-social behaviour Highways and roads Planning Bins and littering School placements Adults' and children’s social services ​ You can find out who your councillor is by entering your postcode here. ​ ​ Surgery Appointments ​ ​ It's often not necessary to wait for a surgery appointment to receive assistance from us. We have a dedicated team of expert caseworkers who support Simon and are ready to help you with a wide range of issues. Whether you need advice, support with a specific problem, or guidance on navigating government services, our caseworkers are here to provide timely and effective assistance. Don't hesitate to reach out to us for help—we're committed to serving you as efficiently as possible. ​ ​ No events at the moment About you Before you begin you should check that Simon is your MP as there is a strict parliamentary protocol which means that MPs can only assist members of their own constituencies. Even if an issue arose elsewhere you should still contact your home MP. You can check who your MP is by entering your postcode on the UK Parliament website . We also have a more comprehensive Casework Form which guides you through everything we may need to assist you and allows you to upload supporting documents. Click here to complete that form i

Turn left onto Westgate and continue straight. Turn right onto Westmorland Street. ​ The office will be on your left. It's a short walk, taking about 5 minutes.​ ​ Walking from Wakefield Westgate: ​ Head southeast on Mulberry Way towards Westgate. Turn left onto Westgate and continue straight. Turn right onto Westmorland Street. ​ The walk should take about 5-7 minutes. ​ Walking from Wakefield Kirkgate: ​ Head northwest on Monk Street towards Kirkgate. Turn left onto Kirkgate and continue straight. Turn right onto Westmorland Street. ​ The walk should take about 10-12 minutes. ​ Parking ​ ​ The Ridings Shopping Centre Car Park (George Street, WF1 1DS) Merchant Gate Car Park (Mulberry Way, WF1 2QN). Turton Street Car Park (WF1 4DR) ​ ​ You can also find more options and book spaces in advance using services like Parkopedia or JustPark ​​ Disabled parking ​ The Ridings Shopping Centre Car Park (George Street, WF1 1DS): This car park has many disabled spaces and is also the home of Wakefield Shop Mobility Borough Road Car Park (Borough Road, WF1 3AZ): This car park has around a dozen disabled spaces On-street parking: Blue Badge holders can park in the bay at the top of Westmorland Street (outside The Raven pub) or in one of the several on-street parking spaces on Wood Street Email You can email Simon at simon.lightwood.mp@parliament.uk ​ © 2026 Simon Lightwood MP | Privacy Policy | Work for Simon | Office Standards | Promoted by Simon Lightwood c/o Yorkshire & the Humber Labour, Quayside House, Canal Wharf, Leeds, LS11 5PS bottom of page

pages.title: Social media ban for children? | Simon Lightwood MP
headings: h2: Your Views on young people and Social Media.
pages.text_excerpt: --> Social media ban for children? | Simon Lightwood MP top of page Home About Advice & Support Visit Parliament Gallery Transport Contact More Use tab to navigate through the menu items. Should we ban social media for under-16's? Download How can we build a healthier, safer relationship with phones and social media. I’m really keen to hear your views on an issue many parents and young people have raised with me: how we can help young people build a healthier, safer relationship with phones and social media. The Government has launched a national conversation on children’s online safety, recognising both the huge benefits of technology and the growing concerns about its impact on young people’s wellbeing. There’s no single agreed solution yet, and a range of options are being explored from setting a minimum age for social media and improving age‑verification, to reviewing the digital age of consent, limiting addictive features like infinite scrolling, and strengthening parental controls. While this work takes place, new guidance is already being rolled out, including screen‑time advice for parents and updated rules to make schools phone‑free by default. I want to make sure the voices of families here in Wakefield and Rothwell are heard in this process, so please do share your thoughts, concerns, and experiences with me. Your insight will help shape the conversations and decisions ahead. Download Jump to survey Key options being considered. The Government is asking for people’s views on several ideas to help manage children’s use of social media. These include: ​ Setting a suitable minimum age for social media, including the possibility of a ban for younger children. ​ Improving age‑verification tools so platforms can better enforce age limits and ensure children only see age‑appropriate content. Reviewing the digital age of consent to decide whether the current threshold is too low. ​ Limiting features that encourage addictive use , such as endless or “infinite” scrolling. ​ Providing more support for parents , including clearer guidance and simpler parental controls to help families navigate the online world. Download Jump to survey Your Views on young people and Social Media.
